_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). OB/GYN: Eclampsia/Pre-Eclampsia. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — OB/GYN: Eclampsia/Pre-Eclampsia

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — OB/GYN: Eclampsia/Pre-Eclampsia
points: Recognize serious conditions associated with pregnancy and hypertension
Prevention of eclampsia-related seizures
Provide adequate treatment for eclampsia-related seizures

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Female patient, more than 20-weeks' gestation, presenting with hypertension and evidence of end organ dysfunction including renal insufficiency, liver involvement, neurological, or hematological involvement
May occur up to 6 weeks postpartum but is rare after 48 hours post-delivery a. Often the presenting symptom of postpartum pre-eclampsia is headache or SOB
Severe features of pre-eclampsia include: a. Severe hypertension (SBP greater than 160, DBP greater than 110)
Eclampsia a. Any pregnant patient who is seizing should be assumed to have eclampsia and treated as such until arrival at the hospital
Eclampsia/pre-eclampsia can be associated with abruptio placenta and fetal loss
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Magnesium toxicity (progression) a. Hypotension followed by
Treatment of magnesium toxicity a. Stop magnesium drip

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Delivery of the placenta is the only definitive management for pre-eclampsia and eclampsia

Early treatment of severe pre-eclampsia with magnesium for seizure prophylaxis and anti-hypertensive significantly reduces the rate of eclampsia. Use of magnesium encouraged if signs of severe pre-eclampsia present to prevent seizure
Patients with a history of chronic hypertension may have superimposed pre-eclampsia
Although less frequent, eclampsia, including eclampsia-related seizures, can occur in postpartum patients
Vital signs assessment with repeat blood pressure monitoring before and after treatment
Assessment of deep tendon reflexes after magnesium therapy
Examination for end organ involvement
Evaluate fundal height

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: Document full vital signs and physical exam findings

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
